They are a series of traps written in book form, the form chosen deliberately, because books imply knowledge and knowledge implies improvement. These do not improve the reader. The author understood this. The author did it anyway. That is all the lore the Book of Pestilence offers. It is enough.</div></div>
              <div class="item-notes">⚠ Inflicts Bleeding immediately on reading. Effect cannot be blocked by armour. Treat with Green Herb compounds or Betadine.</div>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="item-entry caligo-item" data-category="book">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/materials_skill_book_of_pestilence_ii.png" alt="Book of Pestilence II"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Book of Pestilence II</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-book">Pestilence</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-stats"><span class="stat-pill">Type <span>Status Book</span></span><span class="stat-pill">Effect <span>Infection</span></span></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">Reading inflicts Infection status. Infection is a slower-acting status than Bleeding, it does not cause immediate HP loss but instead reduces the effectiveness of all healing items by 50% and incrementally degrades DEF values over time. Treated with Betadine or Antibiotics. If left untreated through multiple encounters, Infection escalates to a more severe compound status.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Description</div><div class="item-section-text">Causes infection when read.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">Of the eight volumes, Infection is the one most consistent with the Church's documented methods. The Underground Laboratory's records include infection as an experimental vector, studied, catalogued, and weaponized in controlled conditions. The Book of Pestilence II did not emerge from nowhere. It emerged from notes. Someone read those notes and decided to put what they learned into a form anyone could pick up.</div></div>
              <div class="item-notes">⚠ Inflicts Infection on reading. Reduces healing efficiency. Treat with Betadine or Antibiotics before escalation.</div>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="item-entry caligo-item" data-category="book">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/materials_skill_book_of_pestilence_iii.png" alt="Book of Pestilence III"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Book of Pestilence III</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-book">Pestilence</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-stats"><span class="stat-pill">Type <span>Status Book</span></span><span class="stat-pill">Effect <span>Broken Bones</span></span></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">Inflicts Broken Bones on reading. This status removes the Sprint ability if learned, halves movement speed, and imposes a damage penalty on physical melee attacks due to compromised striking form. Unlike Bleeding or Infection, Broken Bones has no single-item cure, it requires resting at a safe zone and is one of the only statuses not addressable through inventory management alone.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Description</div><div class="item-section-text">Causes broken bones when read.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">The most physically visceral of the pestilence statuses. A book that breaks bones through the act of reading it operates in the same territory as the Fragments of Caligo, reality responding to attention in ways that have no precedent in natural physics. The Pestilence series was not written by the Church. The Underground Laboratory records reference "text-form status delivery" as an experimental approach. The experiment produced results.</div></div>
              <div class="item-notes">⚠ Broken Bones cannot be cured with inventory items. Requires rest at a safe zone. Avoid equipping before major encounters.</div>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="item-entry caligo-item" data-category="book">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/materials_skill_book_of_pestilence_iv.png" alt="Book of Pestilence IV"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Book of Pestilence IV</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-book">Pestilence</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-stats"><span class="stat-pill">Type <span>Status Book</span></span><span class="stat-pill">Effect <span>Stun</span></span></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">Inflicts Stun immediately on reading. Stun locks all player inputs for a fixed duration, during this window the character cannot dodge, attack, use items, or interact with the environment. The duration is long enough to be dangerous in any active combat zone. Reading this book in a safe area and waiting out the stun is the only responsible approach.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Description</div><div class="item-section-text">Causes stun when read.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">The Stun the book delivers is physical, not psychological, not metaphorical. The words on the page produce a neurological response in the reader that interrupts motor function. This is the point at which the Pestilence series shifts from harmful-but-explicable to something the game's internal logic struggles to contain. Knowledge that directly causes paralysis is not knowledge functioning normally. It is something that uses reading as a delivery mechanism for something else entirely.</div></div>
              <div class="item-notes">⚠ Stun takes effect instantly. Do not read in active combat zones. Wait out the duration in a cleared area.</div>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="item-entry caligo-item" data-category="book">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/materials_skill_book_of_pestilence_v.png" alt="Book of Pestilence V"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Book of Pestilence V</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-book">Pestilence</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-stats"><span class="stat-pill">Type <span>Status Book</span></span><span class="stat-pill">Effect <span>Confusion</span></span></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">Inflicts Confusion on reading. Confusion randomizes directional inputs, movement commands are remapped unpredictably for the duration of the status. Additionally, item use becomes unreliable: there is a chance that using an item while Confused activates the wrong item from the inventory. MS accumulation rate increases during Confusion as a secondary effect.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Description</div><div class="item-section-text">Causes confusion when read.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">Confusion is Caligo's most recognizable signature, the entity operates by disrupting the coherence of meaning, and the Book of Pestilence V delivers a miniaturized version of that disruption through text. The Underground Laboratory experimented with Caligo-derived disorientation as a control mechanism. The book is the result of someone taking that experiment and making it portable. The experiment was classified. The book is not.</div></div>
              <div class="item-notes">⚠ Confusion remaps directional inputs and disrupts item use. MS increases during the status duration. Do not read in proximity to enemies.</div>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="item-entry caligo-item" data-category="book">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/materials_skill_book_of_pestilence_vi.png" alt="Book of Pestilence VI"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Book of Pestilence VI</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-book">Pestilence</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-stats"><span class="stat-pill">Type <span>Status Book</span></span><span class="stat-pill">Effect <span>Blindness</span></span></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">Inflicts Blindness on reading. The game's visual output is partially obscured for the duration, the peripheral view darkens and direct-line visibility shortens significantly. Enemy indicators do not appear through the blindness effect. The character can still function but relies entirely on audio cues and memory of the environment. This is the most disorienting status in the series from a player experience standpoint.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Description</div><div class="item-section-text">Causes blindness when read.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">There is something specific about blindness caused by reading, the organ that processed the text becomes the organ that is damaged by it. The Pestilence series has a pattern of this: the mechanism of the harm and the mechanism of delivery are the same. You use your hands to open the book. You use your eyes to read it. The book uses your eyes to take something from you. This is deliberate. Someone designed this. That fact is not discussed by any character in the game.</div></div>
              <div class="item-notes">⚠ Blindness significantly reduces visibility. Enemy indicators are disabled. Audio cues become the primary navigation tool during the status duration.</div>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="item-entry caligo-item" data-category="book">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/materials_skill_book_of_pestilence_vii.png" alt="Book of Pestilence VII"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Book of Pestilence VII</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-book">Pestilence</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-stats"><span class="stat-pill">Type <span>Status Book</span></span><span class="stat-pill">Effect <span>Critical</span></span></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">Inflicts Critical status on reading. Critical places the character in a state where any additional damage, from any source of any amount, triggers a critical health threshold response: HP is immediately reduced to 1. The Critical status is the most dangerous in the series from a death-risk perspective; a single incidental hit becomes a near-death event. Blue Gems can suppress the effect but not remove it.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Description</div><div class="item-section-text">Causes critical when read.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">The Critical status is not a physical wound, it is a change in the body's relationship to being struck. Something in the text recalibrates what a hit means. This is the closest the Pestilence series comes to operating at a metaphysical level rather than a physical one. Whatever Caligo does to the minds of those it reaches through the Fragment encounters, the Book of Pestilence VII does a version of the same thing to the body's damage threshold. The entity's influence has multiple vocabularies.</div></div>
              <div class="item-notes">⚠ Any damage taken while Critical triggers immediate near-death. Blue Gems suppress but do not remove the status. Avoid all combat until the status clears.</div>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="item-entry caligo-item" data-category="book">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/materials_skill_book_of_pestilence_viii.png" alt="Book of Pestilence VIII"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Book of Pestilence VIII</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-book">Pestilence</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-stats"><span class="stat-pill">Type <span>Status Book</span></span><span class="stat-pill">Effect <span>Infected</span></span></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">Reading inflicts Infected — the compound, escalated form of Infection, distinct in that it cannot be treated with Betadine or Antibiotics alone. Infected requires the combination of Green Herb, Blue Herb, and Empty Vial to produce the compound remedy. It compounds the effects of Infection with an MS accumulation increase and removes the effectiveness of all HP regeneration during its duration.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Description</div><div class="item-section-text">Causes infected when read.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">The eighth volume is the culmination. Each of the seven preceding books delivered a discrete harm. The Infected status compounds and escalates, it is the series' acknowledgment that the individual harms were practice. Whatever the Pestilence books are, they build toward something. The eighth is not a conclusion. It is an arrival point for damage that was accumulating since the first volume. Finding all eight in sequence and reading them, something the game does not prevent, produces a character state the designers specifically named. They named it Infected. They meant something by it beyond the physical.</div></div>
              <div class="item-notes">⚠ Infected requires a compound remedy — Green Herb + Blue Herb + Empty Vial. Standard medicines are insufficient. MS increases during duration. HP regeneration is disabled.</div>
            </div>
          </div>



        </div>
      </div>

      <!-- MATERIALS -->
      <div class="category-group" id="materials">
        <div class="category-label">
          <span class="category-label-text">◇ Materials &amp; Crafting Components</span>
          <div class="category-label-line"></div>
        </div>
        <div class="item-cards-grid">

          <div class="item-entry" data-category="material">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/materials_gem_blue_gem.png" alt="Blue Gem"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Blue Gem</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-material">Material</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">The game's primary MS reducer. Critical to stockpile before entering doubled-rate zones like the Home Village. The Shaman's playthrough begins with one in the Healing Hut, its placement is the game's quiet acknowledgment that Pluvia's starting MS of 30 is already precarious. Not having Blue Gems when the MS escalation hits is the most common cause of Path C triggers.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Description</div><div class="item-section-text">A blue-colored gem of unknown mineral composition. Its calming effect is not metaphorical, it produces a measurable decrease in MS that no in-game character can explain and no Church document accounts for. Caligo is affected by it the same way weather is affected by a clear window: it does not stop, but something about the barrier makes it matter less.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">Blue Gems appear naturally in Tribe territory and near coastal regions outside Church jurisdiction. They do not appear near the Theocratic Capital, the Underground Laboratory, or Cultist Island. Whether this is geological coincidence or something more purposeful is not stated in any document. Pluvia, if asked directly, says: <em>"Some things resist without choosing to. The gem is one of them."</em> She says it the way someone says something they believe is true but cannot prove.</div></div>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="item-entry" data-category="material">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/materials_bar_iron_ingot.png" alt="Iron Ingot"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Iron Ingot</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-material">Material</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">Primary crafting material for weapon upgrades. Two Iron Ingots plus Silver Wire upgrades the Blunderbuss to its Reinforced variant. Found in the Blacksmith Workshop's locked cabinet and dropped by Holy Knights and Church Soldiers. The most common crafting component across all upgrade trees.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">The Church's foundries run continuously. Iron comes from mines in the northern territories, the same territories where the Home Village sits. The village that was destroyed provided iron ore to the Church before it became a site of Eldritch Horror incursion. The supply chain continued for two years after the village was abandoned. The Church marked it as a "supply disruption" in their records, not a tragedy.</div></div>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="item-entry" data-category="material">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/materials_component_silver_wire.png" alt="Silver Wire"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Silver Wire</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-material">Material</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">Used in accessory crafting and the Blunderbuss upgrade. Dropped by Church Assassins, the most elite and dangerous of the Church's mobile operatives. The fact that assassins carry refined silver wire is not coincidence; it is part of their toolkit for weapon modification in the field.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">Silver has traditional significance in Lumen doctrine, associated with clarity and precision, qualities the Church values in instruments that do its work quietly. Church Assassins are not listed in any official Church roster. They are funded through the same administrative category that covers "Charitable Operations." The Silver Wire they carry is listed as a "blessed implement." The Church has creative language for most things.</div></div>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="item-entry" data-category="material">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/materials_foliage_green_herb.png" alt="Green Herb"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Green Herb</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-material">Material</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">Crafting material said to cure infections. Combined with Empty Vials for medicinal compounds. Found commonly in Tribe territory and scattered through the Zones of Terrors, nature persisting in corrupted places, which the game uses as consistent background symbolism.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">The Tribe's Shaman has used Green Herbs in her practice for decades. The Church's medical staff filed a report fourteen years ago recommending the standardization of Tribe herbal knowledge into Church medical protocol. The report was rejected. The rejection note reads: <em>"Sourcing through non-institutional channels creates accountability gaps."</em> People in the Home Village could have used the herbs. They were not given access to the protocol.</div></div>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="item-entry" data-category="material">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/materials_gem_red_gem.png" alt="Red Gem"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Red Gem</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-material">Material</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">Said to contain the soul of a great warrior. Rare drop in high-risk zones. Used in accessory crafting, specifically in combinations that amplify offensive capability at the cost of psychological stability. The trade-off is mechanical and also thematic.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">The Red Gem does not contain a soul. It contains a resonance, a pattern from something that was alive that impressed itself into the crystal's structure at the moment of that thing's death. Whether that qualifies as a soul is the kind of question the Cult's Library of Caligo addresses in seventeen competing marginal notes from seven different readers, none of whom agree, all of whom have circled the same passage in the original text.</div></div>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="item-entry" data-category="material">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/materials_scrap_leather_scraps.png" alt="Leather Scraps"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Leather Scraps</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-material">Material</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">Common crafting material for armor repairs and upgrades. Dropped by Church Soldiers and found throughout the Walled City. Required for the Iron Spear upgrade alongside Iron Ingots.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">Church Soldiers patch their own equipment in the field using Leather Scraps from a standard-issue repair kit. This was not always policy, previously, soldiers returned to armories for repairs. The change was made after too many soldiers used armory visits to speak to blacksmiths like Conrad. Conrad, notably, still has his old appointment logs. He has kept them for twenty years. He is the kind of man who keeps things.</div></div>
            </div>
          </div>


          <div class="item-entry" data-category="material">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/materials_sheet_paper.png" alt="Paper"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Paper</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-material">Material</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-stats"><span class="stat-pill">Type <span>Material</span></span></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">Basic crafting material — used in the production of written items and in recipes requiring a blank substrate. Distinct from Ancient Paper, which is a singular item with fixed lore significance. Standard Paper is a common drop and can be found throughout the world in civilian locations. Multiple units stack in inventory.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Description</div><div class="item-section-text">A blank piece of paper.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">Paper in Elucidate is not neutral. Everything that has been done to people in this world was first written on paper, the Church's directives, the Laboratory's experiment logs, the suppressed reports, the procurement orders for the Home Village's resources that continued after the village was abandoned. Blank paper is just the space before that. It is the only truly innocent material in the crafting system. What is made from it may not be.</div></div>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="item-entry" data-category="material">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/materials_ore_raw_iron.png" alt="Raw Iron"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Raw Iron</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-material">Material</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-stats"><span class="stat-pill">Type <span>Material</span></span></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">Precursor to Iron Ingot, Raw Iron must be processed at Conrad's forge before it can be used in weapon and armor crafting recipes. Found in mine locations and northern territory zones. Not directly usable in any recipe in its raw state. The processing step at the forge is intentional, it represents a crafting pipeline rather than instant availability of refined materials.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Description</div><div class="item-section-text">A raw iron found in the wild.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">The northern territories where Raw Iron is found are the same territories as the Home Village. The mines predated the village, and the village grew around the mining work. When the village was abandoned, the mines were not, the Church contracted outside labor to continue extraction. The Raw Iron in the player's inventory may have come from a mine worked by someone who replaced a person no longer able to work it. That transaction is not documented anywhere.</div></div>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="item-entry" data-category="material">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/materials_plank_wooden_plank.png" alt="Wooden Plank"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Wooden Plank</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-material">Material</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-stats"><span class="stat-pill">Type <span>Material</span></span></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">Primary crafting component for the Wooden Buckler and several environmental interaction recipes. Found abundantly in building sites, abandoned structures, and woodcutting locations. The most accessible structural material in the game, early-game crafting relies on Wooden Plank combinations when iron is not yet available.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Description</div><div class="item-section-text">A piece of wooden plank.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">The Home Village's primary export was lumber processed into planks for Church construction in the capital. The Iron Axes found in the village were used for this work. Wooden Planks from that village went into the walls of buildings in the Theocratic Capital, buildings the player walks through later in the game. The game does not annotate this. It does not need to. The plank is in the wall. The village is gone. The building is standing.</div></div>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="item-entry" data-category="material">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/material_component_bow_string.png" alt="Bowstring"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Bowstring</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-material">Material</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-stats"><span class="stat-pill">Type <span>Material</span></span></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">Required component for crafting and upgrading bow-class weapons. A Short Bow requires one Bowstring plus a Stick and additional components. The Bowstring's fiber composition affects the final weapon's draw-weight rating — higher-quality fibers produce bows with faster arrow velocity. Found in hunting supply caches and Tribe territory storage locations.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Description</div><div class="item-section-text">A fiber made to be used as a spring for arrows.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">The Tribe's bowstrings were made from plant fiber processed through a specific treatment the Church's armory never successfully replicated. Church-produced bowstrings break at higher frequency under the same load. The Tribe knew this. The Church's armory reports documented this. The reports recommended acquiring Tribe manufacturing knowledge through formal exchange. The recommendation was not acted on. The Church continued to use inferior bowstrings. Some things are ideologically expensive to fix.</div></div>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="item-entry" data-category="material">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/materials_foliage_blue_herb.png" alt="Blue Herb"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Blue Herb</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-material">Material</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-stats"><span class="stat-pill">Type <span>Material</span></span></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">Rarer than the Green Herb and required specifically for the compound remedy against the Infected status, one of two herbs required alongside an Empty Vial. Also used in crafting MS-reduction compounds when processed with Blue Gem material. Found in Tribe territory and high-altitude zones outside Church jurisdiction. The Shaman's extended dialogue references it by a different name in Tribe language.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Description</div><div class="item-section-text">A plant that is said to be able to cure all your fatigues.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">Fatigue in Elucidate is not a singular condition, it is the accumulation of everything the game world does to the characters who move through it. The Blue Herb does not cure that. The description says it is <em>said</em> to, the hedge is deliberate. No plant cures everything. The Tribe knows this better than the description suggests. Pluvia, asked about the Blue Herb in extended dialogue, says what it actually does is make the body remember it has rested, even when it has not. That is not nothing.</div></div>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="item-entry" data-category="material">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/materials_component_stick.png" alt="Stick"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Stick</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-material">Material</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-stats"><span class="stat-pill">Type <span>Material</span></span></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">Base crafting component for the Makeshift Spear and Short Bow crafting trees. The most abundant material in the game, found on the ground in any forested or outdoor zone. Its ubiquity means early-game crafting is never gated by availability of this component. Several crafting chains begin here and scale upward: Stick becomes weapon becomes upgrade.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Description</div><div class="item-section-text">A Stick.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">The inventory description is capitalized and nothing else. A Stick. The inventory text system applies the same descriptive effort to a Stick as it does to the Fragments of Caligo, both get an entry, both get a description. The Stick's description is three characters plus a period. This is either the writer reaching the end of their patience or a deliberate comment on the economy of attention: not everything needs its story told. Some things are just sticks. The world contains them. You pick them up.</div></div>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="item-entry" data-category="material">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/materials_container_empty_vial.png" alt="Empty Vial"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Empty Vial</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-material">Material</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-stats"><span class="stat-pill">Type <span>Material</span></span></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">Container component required for any liquid-based crafting recipe. Essential for the Infected compound remedy, without an Empty Vial, the Green Herb and Blue Herb cannot be combined into a usable form. Also used in producing custom MS-reduction compounds in later crafting tiers. Found in the Underground Laboratory, apothecary locations, and Tribe storage.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Description</div><div class="item-section-text">An empty glass vial.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">The Underground Laboratory produced a significant quantity of empty vials, the experiment logs reference hundreds of compounds tested in glass vessels. Most of those vessels were destroyed with their contents after each experiment. Some were not. The Empty Vials in the game world that come from the Laboratory carry the residue of whatever was last in them. The residue is not visible. The crafting system does not differentiate between a clean vial and one that has held something that should not have existed. Perhaps it should.</div></div>
            </div>
          </div>

        </div>
      </div>

      <!-- POTIONS -->
      <div class="category-group" id="potions">
        <div class="category-label">
          <span class="category-label-text">⚗ Potions &amp; Consumables</span>
          <div class="category-label-line"></div>
        </div>
        <div class="item-cards-grid">

          <div class="item-entry" data-category="potion">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/materials_potion_red_vial.png" alt="Red Vial"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Red Vial</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-potion">Potion</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-stats"><span class="stat-pill">Effect <span>HP +70</span></span></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">Powerful instant HP recovery. The most efficient healing item in the game, 70 HP restored immediately with no side effects. Scarce in early zones; more available after the Port City. Essential stockpile item for the Cultist Island assault.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">Church medical reports refer to Red Vials as <em>accelerated coagulant compound with neural stabilizing properties</em>. The same compound appears in the Underground Laboratory's supply manifests listed under a different name: <em>Subject Stabilization Fluid</em>. The applications differ. The formula is identical. The Church is meticulous about language in exactly the places where language is doing the most work.</div></div>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="item-entry" data-category="potion">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/materials_beverage_ale.png" alt="Ale"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Ale / Rum / Wine</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-potion">Drink</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-stats"><span class="stat-pill">Effect <span>MS -10</span></span></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">All three alcohol variants reduce MS by 10. A tactical option in zones where Blue Gems have been depleted. The Tavern Keeper at The Gilded Anchor keeps them stocked and notices who drinks what quantity, his tracking of Konrad's dark ale consumption is how he knows Konrad is carrying something heavy.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">The game acknowledges without judgment that alcohol as a coping mechanism for psychological pressure is both understandable and insufficient. Ale reduces MS. It does not resolve what caused the MS to rise. The Tavern Keeper, who has observed this dynamic for twenty years, puts Konrad's second drink on the house. He does not say this is because he cares. He does not need to say it.</div></div>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="item-entry" data-category="potion">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/materials_potion_antibiotics.png" alt="Betadine"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Betadine / Antibiotics</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-potion">Medicine</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-stats"><span class="stat-pill">Effect <span>Remove Infection</span></span></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">Both items cure the Infection status — Betadine for wound cleaning, Antibiotics for systemic infection. The Zones of Terrors have environmental hazards that inflict Infection through contact with corrupted surfaces. Having either item available is essential in high-risk exploration.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">The Tribe's healing traditions use neither. They use Green Herbs, specific clay, and Pluvia's guidance. The Church brought Betadine and Antibiotics into common use, genuine medical contributions that made people's lives better. This is acknowledged in the game's lore and not used to excuse anything. Good work and terrible work can come from the same institution. That is part of what makes the institution difficult to simply condemn and leave.</div></div>
            </div>
          </div>

        </div>
      </div>

      <!-- ACCESSORIES -->
      <div class="category-group" id="accessories">
        <div class="category-label">
          <span class="category-label-text">💎 Accessories</span>
          <div class="category-label-line"></div>
        </div>
        <div class="item-cards-grid">

          <div class="item-entry" data-category="accessory">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/armour_accessories_blue_amulet.png" alt="Blue Amulet"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Blue Amulet</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-accessory">Accessory</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-stats"><span class="stat-pill">Mind Res <span>+1</span></span></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">Passive Mind Resistance increase. Worn, it slows MS accumulation throughout the playthrough, modest but consistent. Particularly useful for Funeris and Konrad, whose base MS resistance is 3/3, the lowest in the game.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">Strange sense of calm. That is the item description's full text. In a game that documents everything in clinical or literary detail, the Blue Amulet has seven words of description and no lore entry in any document. The Tribe Elder, if asked about it, says only: <em>"Some things do not explain themselves. They simply are."</em> This is either profound or the game acknowledging it ran out of origin text for one item. The calm is real either way.</div></div>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="item-entry" data-category="accessory">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/armour_accessories_ring.png" alt="Ring"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Ring</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-accessory">Accessory</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-stats"><span class="stat-pill">DEF <span>1</span></span></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">Minimal stats — 1 defense. But equipping it triggers a passive event flag that unlocks optional NPC dialogue across multiple zones, specifically from characters who recognize what the ring is. What it is, is not explained unless those conversations are found.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">Item description: <em>A promise you failed to fulfil. Maybe you can still try again.</em> The ring has no crafting origin and no purchase source, it is found, each time in a different place depending on which character's playthrough is active. A promise made before the game begins. The person it was made to is not identified. The game trusts the player to carry the weight of not knowing and not puts it down.</div></div>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="item-entry" data-category="accessory">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/armour_accessories_red_scarf.png" alt="Red Scarf"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Red Scarf</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-accessory">Head</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-stats"><span class="stat-pill">DEF <span>1</span></span></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">1 defense. Categorized as a head item because the game's equipment system requires it, but it does not function as a helmet in any conventional sense. Wearing it in the Orphanage ruins or the Home Village triggers a unique, brief animation that plays once per playthrough and cannot be triggered again.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">Item description: <em>Reminds you of a distant past.</em> The Red Scarf and the Black Dress are the game's two memory items, objects with no combat value that function entirely as emotional prompts. The Scarf's distant past belongs to whoever is holding it. The game does not specify. It does not need to. What matters is that the character holds something familiar enough to hurt and does not put it down.</div></div>
            </div>
          </div>
		  
          <div class="item-entry" data-category="accessory">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/armour_accessories_swift_boots.png" alt="Swift Boots"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Swift Boots</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-accessory">Accessory</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-stats"><span class="stat-pill">DEF <span>4</span></span><span class="stat-pill">SPD <span>+1</span></span><span class="stat-pill">Type <span>Accessory</span></span></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">The only accessory providing a speed bonus. The SPD +1 modifier increases dodge window frames and sprint distance per stamina unit. Minimal defense as a secondary benefit. Essential for builds relying on Agility Augmentation and Escape skill interactions, the speed bonus stacks with both skill effects for a combined mobility profile unlike any other loadout.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Description</div><div class="item-section-text">You feel faster running around.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">The inventory description is written in second person, one of only a few items in the game where the text speaks directly to the wearer rather than describing the item. The boots are not remarkable in appearance. What is remarkable is how they feel. Some things are only legible from inside them. The game understands that certain knowledge is only available to the person currently running.</div></div>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="item-entry" data-category="accessory">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/material_toy_black_dressed_doll.png" alt="Black Dressed Doll"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Black Dressed Doll</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-accessory">Accessory</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-stats"><span class="stat-pill">Type <span>Accessory</span></span></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">No stats. Equipping it alongside the Black Dress triggers a suppressed memory cutscene that is otherwise inaccessible. Certain NPCs react differently when the player carries the doll, dialogue options open that do not appear otherwise. The doll occupies an accessory slot without providing any mechanical benefit, meaning its value is entirely in what it unlocks rather than what it does.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Description</div><div class="item-section-text">There's a strange sense of familiarity when holding this doll.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">The doll and the Black Dress belong to the same person, or to the same memory of a person, the game is deliberate about leaving this ambiguous. Familiarity is not the same as memory. The doll is familiar the way grief is familiar: not because you recognize the specific thing, but because some part of you already knows the shape of it. The character who carries the doll has not been given language for what it means to them. The doll carries it anyway.</div></div>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="item-entry" data-category="accessory">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/armour_accessories_red_amulet.png" alt="Red Amulet"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Red Amulet</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-accessory">Accessory</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-stats"><span class="stat-pill">DMG <span>+3</span></span><span class="stat-pill">Type <span>Accessory</span></span></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">Offensive accessory providing a flat damage bonus to all attacks regardless of weapon type. Pairs with the Red Gem material, lore-consistent items that share a lineage. The +3 bonus applies before enemy resistances, making it more valuable against lightly armored targets where the full bonus translates cleanly. The only accessory providing pure offensive enhancement without defensive trade-offs.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Description</div><div class="item-section-text">You feel slightly stronger wearing this.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">The Red Gem material is described as containing the soul of a great warrior. The Red Amulet, presumably made from or related to a Red Gem, passes something of that forward. Whether what is passed is strength in any meaningful sense, or simply the belief in strength, the game does not differentiate. The result is the same. <em>You feel slightly stronger</em> is either a metaphor or a mechanic. In Elucidate, both are true at the same time.</div></div>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="item-entry" data-category="accessory">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/armour_armwear_arm_guard.png" alt="Arm Guard"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Arm Guard</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-accessory">Accessory</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-stats"><span class="stat-pill">DEF <span>13</span></span><span class="stat-pill">Mind Res <span>+1</span></span><span class="stat-pill">Type <span>Accessory</span></span></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">The only accessory combining physical defense with Mind State resistance. DEF 13 as an accessory is unusually high, paired with a body and head slot armour, it contributes meaningfully to overall protection. The Mind Res +1 reduces MS accumulation rate in Caligo-influenced zones. Critical for sustained exploration of the Underground Laboratory and inner sanctum areas.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Description</div><div class="item-section-text">You feel much more protected.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">Protection that works on two levels simultaneously. The physical guard is obvious, the arm is covered, the arm is harder to damage. The Mind Resistance is less obvious: whatever material the Arm Guard is made from has a property that the Church's records classify under <em>anomalous material behavior</em> and then do not elaborate on. Anomalous. That word appears seventeen times in Church documents related to items that resist Caligo. They never define it. They never explain why they keep using it.</div></div>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="item-entry" data-category="accessory">
            <div class="item-img-wrap"><img src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/armour_accessories_blue_amulet.png" alt="Wind Essence"><span class="item-symbol"></span><div class="item-img-overlay"></div></div>
            <div class="item-body">
              <div class="item-header">
                <div class="item-name-block"><div class="item-name">Wind Essence</div></div>
                <span class="item-type-badge badge-accessory">Relic</span>
              </div>
              <div class="item-stats"><span class="stat-pill">Type <span>Companion Relic</span></span></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Function</div><div class="item-section-text">Given by the Tribe Chief when Pluvia chooses to accompany the Mercenary. Not a standard equipment item, it cannot be equipped or discarded. It occupies a unique inventory slot labeled <em>carried</em>. Its presence affects Pluvia's Foresight accuracy and provides a passive MS resistance effect only when both the Mercenary and Pluvia are in the same zone.</div></div>
              <div class="item-section"><div class="item-section-label">Lore</div><div class="item-section-text lore">The Tribe Chief's words: <em>It is not a gift. It is a promise that we believe in what she is walking toward.</em> The Wind Essence was Pluvia's from the moment she was born, the Tribe held it in trust. It was always hers to carry; they were simply the ones keeping it safe until she chose to leave. That she chose to leave is the whole of what the Essence represents: the moment belonging and purpose point in different directions, and someone moves toward purpose.</div></div>
            </div>
          </div>


        </div>
      </div>

    </section>

    <div class="section-divider"><div class="div-line"></div><div class="div-icon">▸</div><div class="div-line"></div></div>

<script>
(function generateStars() {
  const container = document.getElementById('stars-container');
  if (!container) return;
  for (let i = 0; i < 60; i++) {
    const s = document.createElement('div');
    s.className = 'star';
    s.style.cssText = `
      left: ${Math.random() * 100}%;
      top:  ${Math.random() * 100}%;
      --d:  ${3 + Math.random() * 5}s;
      --o:  ${0.2 + Math.random() * 0.5};
      animation-delay: ${Math.random() * 6}s;
      width:  ${Math.random() < 0.15 ? 2 : 1}px;
      height: ${Math.random() < 0.15 ? 2 : 1}px;
    `;
    container.appendChild(s);
  }
})();

const filterBtns = document.querySelectorAll('.filter-btn');
const itemCountEl = document.getElementById('item-count');

function updateCount() {
  const activeFilter = document.querySelector('.filter-btn.active')?.dataset.filter || 'all';
  const all = document.querySelectorAll('[data-category]');
  let visible = 0;
  all.forEach(el => {
    const show = activeFilter === 'all' || el.dataset.category === activeFilter;
    el.style.display = show ? '' : 'none';
    if (show) visible++;
  });
  itemCountEl.textContent = `${visible} items`;
  document.querySelectorAll('.category-group').forEach(group => {
    const anyVisible = group.querySelectorAll('[data-category]:not([style*="none"])').length > 0;
    group.style.display = anyVisible ? '' : 'none';
  });
}

filterBtns.forEach(btn => {
  btn.addEventListener('click', () => {
    filterBtns.forEach(b => b.classList.remove('active'));
    btn.classList.add('active');
    updateCount();
  });
});
updateCount();

const itemIndex = [];
document.querySelectorAll('[data-category]').forEach(card => {
  const name = card.querySelector('.item-name, .fragment-card-name, .enlightenment-card-name');
  const texts = card.querySelectorAll('.item-section-text, .fragment-section-text, .enlightenment-text');
  if (name) {
    itemIndex.push({
      name: name.textContent.trim(),
      text: Array.from(texts).map(t => t.textContent).join(' ').toLowerCase(),
      el: card
    });
  }
});

const inputEl = document.getElementById('search-input');
const overlayEl = document.getElementById('search-results-overlay');

inputEl.addEventListener('input', () => {
  const q = inputEl.value.trim().toLowerCase();
  overlayEl.innerHTML = '';
  if (!q || q.length < 2) { overlayEl.classList.remove('visible'); return; }
  const results = itemIndex.filter(i => i.name.toLowerCase().includes(q) || i.text.includes(q)).slice(0, 10);
  if (!results.length) {
    overlayEl.innerHTML = '<div class="search-no-result">No items found for "' + q + '"</div>';
  } else {
    results.forEach(item => {
      const div = document.createElement('div');
      div.className = 'search-result-item';
      div.innerHTML = `<div class="sr-title">${highlightMatch(item.name, q)}</div><div class="sr-excerpt">Click to scroll to item</div>`;
      div.onclick = () => {
        overlayEl.classList.remove('visible');
        item.el.scrollIntoView({ behavior: 'smooth', block: 'center' });
        item.el.style.outline = '1px solid var(--gold)';
        setTimeout(() => { item.el.style.outline = ''; }, 2000);
      };
      overlayEl.appendChild(div);
    });
  }
  overlayEl.classList.add('visible');
});

inputEl.addEventListener('keydown', e => { if (e.key === 'Escape') { overlayEl.classList.remove('visible'); inputEl.blur(); } });
document.addEventListener('click', e => { if (!e.target.closest('.search-wrap') && !e.target.closest('#search-results-overlay')) overlayEl.classList.remove('visible'); });

function highlightMatch(text, q) {
  const i = text.toLowerCase().indexOf(q);
  if (i < 0) return text;
  return text.slice(0, i) + '<mark class="search-highlight">' + text.slice(i, i + q.length) + '</mark>' + text.slice(i + q.length);
}

const observer = new IntersectionObserver(entries => {
  entries.forEach(e => {
    if (e.isIntersecting) { e.target.style.opacity = '1'; e.target.style.transform = 'translateY(0)'; }
  });
}, { threshold: 0.06, rootMargin: '0px 0px -30px 0px' });

document.querySelectorAll('.item-entry, .fragment-card, .enlightenment-card').forEach(el => {
  el.style.opacity = '0';
  el.style.transform = 'translateY(18px)';
  el.style.transition = 'opacity 0.45s ease, transform 0.45s ease';
  observer.observe(el);
});
</script>
</body>
</html>

    <section class="lore-section" id="world-lore">
      <div class="section-header">
        <span class="section-tag">Cosmology</span>
        <h2 class="section-title">The <span class="gold">World of Elucidate</span></h2>
        <p class="section-desc">Before light gave the world its name, there was only the formless dark.</p>
      </div>

      <div class="lore-intro-block">

        <div class="lore-chapter reveal">
          <div class="lore-chapter-label">Chapter I — Origin</div>
          <h3 class="lore-chapter-title">Before the First Light: The Age of Caligo</h3>
          <div class="lore-body">
            <p>The world did not begin with creation. It began with <span class="lore-caligo">Caligo</span> — an absence so total, so vast in its formlessness, that to look upon it directly was to feel your own edges dissolve. The ancient texts do not call it evil. They call it <em>precedent</em>. Caligo predated everything: the mountains, the seas, the living things that would one day name themselves and fight wars over those names. It had no will. No preference. It existed the way weather exists, without intention, without malice, and without mercy.</p>
            <p>For ages beyond reckoning, the world breathed in this darkness. Strange organisms adapted to it. Philosophies were built within it. And those who survived long enough in the black developed a relationship with Caligo that was less devotion and more <span class="lore-emphasis">mutual accommodation</span>, you do not worship the tide that swallows you; you simply learn which shores to avoid.</p>
          </div>
          <div class="lore-pull-quote">"The darkness does not hate the candle. It simply persists after the candle goes out." — Recovered inscription, Theocratic Capital Library</div>
          <div class="lore-body">
            <p>Then came light. Not the slow warming of a dawn, but a rupture, a <span class="lore-lumen">Vindication Lumen</span>, a cosmic declaration that illumination was not merely possible but <em>correct</em>. The event fractured the world's relationship with itself. Where Caligo had been the universal medium of existence, it was now a <span class="lore-caligo">contaminant</span>, a thing to be purged, an enemy defined by its opposition to something new.</p>
          </div>
        </div>

        <div class="lore-chapter reveal">
          <div class="lore-chapter-label">Chapter II — The Conflict</div>
          <h3 class="lore-chapter-title">Vindication Lumen and the Church's First Lie</h3>
          <div class="lore-body">
            <p>The Church of Lumen was not built on falsehood. In its earliest form it was a genuine order, scholars and practitioners who had witnessed the Vindication and sought to understand what it meant to live in a world where light and darkness were now in active opposition. They built temples not to control but to <span class="lore-lumen">comprehend</span>. They named the cosmic force Lumen and offered themselves as its intermediaries.</p>
            <p>But comprehension is expensive to maintain. Control is simpler to sell. Over generations, the scholars became administrators. The administrators became generals. The temples became fortresses. By the time the Church declared its first Holy War against the Cult of Caligo, it had so thoroughly inverted its own principles that its holy texts had been quietly rewritten at least four times, each revision granting the ruling body slightly more authority, slightly more justification, slightly more reason why the suffering of others was cosmically sanctioned.</p>
            <p>The Cult of Caligo, by contrast, was never a philosophy. It was <span class="lore-caligo">a consequence</span>. Failed Church experiments. Orphans discarded when they proved insufficient. Soldiers abandoned in zones of eldritch corruption. People who had looked into the dark for so long that the dark had looked back, and offered something the Church never would: acknowledgment of what they truly were.</p>
          </div>
        </div>

        <div class="lore-chapter reveal">
          <div class="lore-chapter-label">Chapter III — The Corruption</div>
          <h3 class="lore-chapter-title">Subject I-3 and the Spread of the Fracture</h3>
          <div class="lore-body">
            <p>The Church's most catastrophic error was not its wars or its doctrine or even its underground laboratories where children were remade into weapons. Its most catastrophic error was <span class="lore-caligo">Subject I-3</span> — the experiment that survived when it should not have, that thrived when it should not have, and that carried within it a piece of Caligo's ancient presence like a wound that never stopped weeping.</p>
            <p>When Subject I-3 escaped containment, it did not flee. It <em>walked</em>. Through the laboratory corridors, through the restricted wings, through the capital, and out into the world. And wherever it moved, the geography of the land shifted slightly, not visibly, not measurably, but in the way a room shifts when someone leaves it who was not supposed to be there at all. Trees leaned at wrong angles. Water ran toward high ground. The sky remembered Caligo and turned colors it had not been permitted to turn since before the first Lumen burned.</p>
            <p>These became the <span class="lore-caligo">Zones of Terrors</span>. Not places Caligo chose, but places that chose themselves, points where the membrane between the world's true nature and its imposed order grew thin enough to see through. The Church declared them blasphemous. The Cult called them sacred. The people who actually lived near them simply moved away and tried not to look back.</p>
          </div>
          <div class="lore-pull-quote">"Every region you will walk through has been shaped by something it cannot explain and cannot forget. That is the nature of this world. That is the nature of you." — Pluvia (Shaman), to the Mercenary</div>
        </div>

      </div>
    </section>

    <div class="section-divider"><div class="div-line"></div><div class="div-icon">✦</div><div class="div-line"></div></div>

    <section class="regions-section">

      <!-- ══ WALLED CITY ══ -->
      <div class="region-entry" id="walled-city">
        <div class="region-header">
          <div class="region-title-block">
            <div class="region-number">Region 01</div>
            <h2 class="region-name">Walled City</h2>
            <div class="region-subtitle">Military Hub — Starting Point (Mercenary)</div>
          </div>
          <div class="region-badges">
            <span class="region-badge badge-safe">Safe Zone</span>
            <span class="region-badge badge-ms">MS: +0</span>
          </div>
        </div>

		<div class="region-img-wrap reveal">
		  <img 
			src="http://elucidate.unaux.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/Copy-of-WALLED-CITY.jpg" 
			alt="Walled City Environment" 
			class="region-img"
		  >
		  
		  <div class="region-img-overlay"></div>
		  <div class="region-img-label">Walled City — Safe Zone</div>
		</div>

        <div class="region-body">
          <div class="region-description">
            <div class="rblock-label">Environment</div>
            <div class="rblock-title">A City Built to Contain</div>
            <div class="atmo-grid">
              <div class="atmo-item"><div class="atmo-key">Palette</div><div class="atmo-val">Grey and brown stone. Torchlit interiors.</div></div>
              <div class="atmo-item"><div class="atmo-key">Lighting</div><div class="atmo-val">Overcast daytime. Torches everywhere.</div></div>
              <div class="atmo-item"><div class="atmo-key">Sounds</div><div class="atmo-val">Metal clanging, war drums distant, crowd murmur.</div></div>
              <div class="atmo-item"><div class="atmo-key">Hazards</div><div class="atmo-val">None. Soldiers create psychological pressure.</div></div>
            </div>
            <div class="rblock-text">
              <p>Imposing stone walls rise at every approach, blacksmith fires glow perpetually orange through iron-barred windows, and every citizen moves with the hurried intentionality of someone who has been told their purpose in life and accepted it. The Walled City is tense but controlled, the calm before a storm that the soldiers can smell coming but have not been given permission to discuss.</p>
              <p>The city serves one function: produce soldiers and equip them. Everything here exists in service of that mandate. The markets sell weapons. The chapels pray for victory. The drafting offices process names like ledger entries. And yet something quiet persists, a blacksmith who asks too many questions, a notice board that hints at things the Church has not sanctioned, a silence between the drum beats that lasts a half-second longer than it should.</p>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="region-lore-block">
            <div class="rblock-label">Lore</div>
            <div class="rblock-title">The Church's Outstretched Fist</div>
            <div class="rblock-text">
              <p>The Walled City predates the current war by at least four generations. Archaeological evidence buried beneath the armory suggests the city was originally a neutral trading post, its walls built for commerce protection, not military staging. The Church absorbed it during the First Expansion without violence, simply by making itself indispensable to the city's economy until the city could not function without Church infrastructure.</p>
              <p>By the time Caligo's influence began spreading through the eastern territories and the Cult of Caligo emerged from the shadows of the Church's own failed experiments, the Walled City had been remade entirely. Its original identity exists only in the names of its streets, names that mean nothing to the soldiers who march through them now, and everything to the very few historians who survived the Church's archival purges.</p>
              <p>Blacksmith Conrad has worked here for thirty years. He knows what the city was. He equips the Mercenary without enthusiasm and watches him leave with the practiced sadness of someone who has equipped too many and seen how few return with their certainties intact.</p>
            </div>
          </div>
        </div>

        <div class="ms-warning">
          <div class="ms-warning-icon">🧠</div>
          <div class="ms-warning-content">
            <div class="ms-warning-title">Mind State Effect</div>
            <div class="ms-warning-text">No Mind State increase in Walled City. It is the baseline calm, the player's psychological zero point. This contrast is deliberate. Everything measured afterward is measured against what the Walled City felt like.</div>
          </div>
        </div>

                <div class="segment-lore">The gate has never in living memory been breached. This fact is repeated often enough that it has begun to sound like a prayer.</div>
