      <div class="region-entry danger-zone" id="the-borderlands">
        <div class="region-header">
          <div class="region-title-block">
            <div class="region-number">Region 10</div>
            <h2 class="region-name">The Borderlands</h2>
            <div class="region-subtitle">War-Torn Front — Contested Territory</div>
          </div>
          <div class="region-badges">
            <span class="region-badge badge-danger">Conflict Zone</span>
            <span class="region-badge badge-ms">MS: ? / min</span>
          </div>
        </div>

        <div class="region-body">
          <div class="region-description">
            <div class="rblock-label">Environment</div>
            <div class="rblock-title">A Land That Belongs to No One</div>
            <div class="atmo-grid">
              <div class="atmo-item"><div class="atmo-key">Palette</div><div class="atmo-val">Ashen grey. Burnt orange. Mud-brown devastation. Distant firelight.</div></div>
              <div class="atmo-item"><div class="atmo-key">Lighting</div><div class="atmo-val">Overcast permanently. Smoke obscures all horizon lines. No clear day.</div></div>
              <div class="atmo-item"><div class="atmo-key">Sounds</div><div class="atmo-val">Distant artillery. Wind through ruined structures. Irregular gunfire. Silence that arrives suddenly and means something worse is coming.</div></div>
              <div class="atmo-item"><div class="atmo-key">Hazards</div><div class="atmo-val">Active crossfire. Territorial instability. No neutral ground. Mind State accelerates near mass death sites.</div></div>
            </div>
            <div class="rblock-text">
              <p>The Borderlands exist in the space between formal authority and outright chaos, a region where the Church's reach has thinned, the Cult's influence bleeds at the edges, and several smaller nations fight over land that all of them have already half-destroyed. Nothing here belongs to anyone for more than a few days at a time. The architecture is transitional: buildings repaired into fortifications, repaired again after destruction, repaired again with whatever material was left standing. Every wall tells a different story in different stone.</p>
              <p>Movement through the Borderlands requires reading the silence. Active fronts announce themselves. The quiet zones are worse, they are quiet because everyone in them has already left, or already died, or is waiting for something to happen that will not be survivable. The Mercenary who passes through here is not a soldier in this war. He is a variable that no faction has accounted for, and that makes him dangerous to all of them equally.</p>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="region-lore-block">
            <div class="rblock-label">Lore</div>
            <div class="rblock-title">The Fracture Between Powers</div>
            <div class="rblock-text">
              <p>The Borderlands were not always contested. Historical records, what survive of them, indicate a network of independent settlements that managed their own trade, owed allegiance to no single power, and had negotiated a functional peace between them for nearly two centuries. The Church of Lumen ended this. Not through direct conquest, but through the same gradual economic absorption that converted the Walled City: making itself necessary until neutrality became impossible, then reclassifying the settlements as Church-adjacent territory when the settlements did not formally object.</p>
              <p>The other nations objected on the settlements' behalf. The Church framed their objection as aggression. The war that followed was not formally declared by anyone. It simply began, in the way that wars begin when every party has decided the cost of not starting it outweighs the cost of starting it. The Borderlands are what remains of the settlements that were caught in between.</p>
              <p>Caligo's influence on the Borderlands is indirect but measurable. Mass death concentrations leave residue that the game's Mind State system responds to. The Mercenary's psychological state deteriorates faster near sites of recent battle, not because Caligo is present, but because trauma leaves an imprint on spaces that something like Caligo can amplify. The Borderlands are not a Caligo zone. They are a human-made catastrophe that Caligo's presence has made measurably worse.</p>
              <p>No fragment of Caligo has been confirmed in the Borderlands. The cultists avoid it. The Church's experiments avoid it. The researchers whose notes survived suggest that the chaotic overlay of competing human intentions creates interference that even Caligo-sensitive instruments cannot cleanly read. The Borderlands are, in this sense, a zone that has created its own form of incomprehensibility, not through supernatural corruption, but through the sheer volume of competing, unresolved histories written into its ground.</p>
            </div>
          </div>
        </div>

        <div class="ms-warning">
          <div class="ms-warning-icon">🧠</div>
          <div class="ms-warning-content">
            <div class="ms-warning-title">Mind State Effect</div>
            <div class="ms-warning-text">???.</div>
          </div>
        </div>

        <div class="items-found reveal">
          <div class="items-found-title">All Items Found in The Borderlands</div>
          <div class="items-grid">
            <span class="item-chip"><span class="chip-icon">📋</span>Contested Territory Reports</span>
          </div>
        </div>
      </div>
	  
	        <div class="region-entry caligo-zone" id="greywood">
        <div class="region-header">
          <div class="region-title-block">
            <div class="region-number">Region Unknown</div>
            <h2 class="region-name">The Greywood</h2>
            <div class="region-subtitle">Caligo-Adjacent Zone — Dark Forest — Mind State Border</div>
          </div>
          <div class="region-badges">
            <span class="region-badge badge-caligo">Caligo-Adjacent</span>
            <span class="region-badge badge-ms">MS: Passive Drain</span>
          </div>
        </div>

        <div class="region-body">
          <div class="region-description">
            <div class="rblock-label">Environment</div>
            <div class="rblock-title">Where the Light Stopped Trying</div>
            <div class="atmo-grid">
              <div class="atmo-item"><div class="atmo-key">Palette</div><div class="atmo-val">Grey-green and near-black. Bioluminescent moss patches.</div></div>
              <div class="atmo-item"><div class="atmo-key">Lighting</div><div class="atmo-val">Perpetual twilight. No direct sky visibility.</div></div>
              <div class="atmo-item"><div class="atmo-key">Sounds</div><div class="atmo-val">Wind through hollow trunks. Deep structural creaking. No birds.</div></div>
              <div class="atmo-item"><div class="atmo-key">Hazards</div><div class="atmo-val">Disorientation. Slow MS passive increase. Zone border patrol.</div></div>
            </div>
            <div class="rblock-text">
              <p>A dense canopy forest occupying the contested border between the Outskirts and the nearest Zone of Terror. The Greywood is not corrupted in any formal classification, its Mind State toll is low enough that the Church has not designated it a high-risk area, and the Cult has not claimed it as territory. It exists between categories, which is precisely what makes it useful to people who need to move between places without being observed by either institution.</p>
              <p>The trees here are centuries old. Their growth patterns suggest they predate the current war, the previous war, and possibly the Church itself. Something in the soil composition, which Church surveyors noted but did not investigate further, produces a faint luminescence in the ground-level moss after rain. The Greywood at night is lit by the ground rather than the sky. This detail appears nowhere in official Church documentation. The surveyors found it unsettling and omitted it from the public report.</p>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="region-lore-block">
            <div class="rblock-label">Lore</div>
            <div class="rblock-title">The Forest That Remembers</div>
            <div class="rblock-text">
              <p>The Greywood's subtle Caligo adjacency is not the result of a single formation event. It accumulated over decades of proximity, the slow absorption of a landscape that sits close enough to genuine corruption that Caligo's ambient presence has seeped into the soil chemistry and canopy patterns without ever producing the dramatic rupture event that would classify it as a Zone. It is, in the Librarian Scholar's framing, an example of corruption working through persistence rather than force: not a wound, but a long, patient stain.</p>
              <p>The Elven communities that once used the Greywood as a border marker have withdrawn deeper into its interior over the past generation. Whether this is avoidance or accommodation is a question the Elves have not answered for anyone who has thought to ask them, which has not been many people.</p>
            </div>
          </div>
        </div>

        <div class="ms-warning">
          <div class="ms-warning-icon">🌑</div>
          <div class="ms-warning-content">
            <div class="ms-warning-title">Mind State Effect</div>
            <div class="ms-warning-text">Unknown</div>
          </div>
        </div>
		</div>
		
	    <div class="region-entry" id="kingdom-of-rhemer">
        <div class="region-header">
          <div class="region-title-block">
            <div class="region-number">Region ???</div>
            <h2 class="region-name">Kingdom of Rhemer</h2>
            <div class="region-subtitle">??? — Records Lost</div>
          </div>
          <div class="region-badges">
            <span class="region-badge" style="background:rgba(80,60,110,0.2);border-color:rgba(140,100,180,0.4);color:#a07ad0;">??? Zone</span>
            <span class="region-badge badge-ms">MS: ???</span>
          </div>
        </div>

        <div class="region-body">
          <div class="region-description">
            <div class="rblock-label">Environment</div>
            <div class="rblock-title">A Kingdom That May Not Exist</div>
            <div class="atmo-grid">
              <div class="atmo-item"><div class="atmo-key">Palette</div><div class="atmo-val">Unrecorded.</div></div>
              <div class="atmo-item"><div class="atmo-key">Lighting</div><div class="atmo-val">Unrecorded.</div></div>
              <div class="atmo-item"><div class="atmo-key">Sounds</div><div class="atmo-val">Unrecorded.</div></div>
              <div class="atmo-item"><div class="atmo-key">Hazards</div><div class="atmo-val">Unknown. Conflicting accounts vary significantly on this point.</div></div>
            </div>
            <div class="rblock-text">
              <p>The Kingdom of Rhemer appears in historical fragments predating the current war by an indeterminate period. References are inconsistent, some place it east of the Borderlands, others west of the Zones of Terrors. Several accounts do not agree on what direction means in the region they describe. Whether this is a cartographic failure, a translation error, or something else is not recorded.</p>
              <p>No verified traveler account places a living observer within its borders. Reports describing Rhemer are secondhand at the earliest. Most are further removed than that. The fragments that do exist agree on almost nothing except the name, and even that appears in three variant spellings across different sources.</p>
            </div>
          </div>

          <div class="region-lore-block">
            <div class="rblock-label">Lore</div>
            <div class="rblock-title">What the Records Did Not Survive to Say</div>
            <div class="rblock-text">
              <p>Records pertaining to the Kingdom of Rhemer were either lost, destroyed, or never committed to writing in any form that survived. The Church archives contain two references: one in an undated margin annotation describing Rhemer as "the predecessor," and one in a destroyed document whose destruction record itself references Rhemer without explaining why the document required destruction.</p>
              <p>The Cult's recovered texts contain a single notation, the word "Rhemer" followed by a symbol that does not correspond to any known notation system in the Cult's documented lexicon. No Cult scholar interviewed on the subject has been able to identify it. Two of those scholars declined to continue the interview upon seeing the symbol written out.</p>
              <p>Whether the Kingdom of Rhemer existed as a political entity, a geographic location, a philosophical designation, or something that does not fit neatly into any of those categories is unknown. Conflicting accounts make it impossible to construct a coherent picture. This may be the nature of Rhemer. It may be something else.</p>
            </div>
            <div class="lore-pull-quote" style="border-color:rgba(140,100,180,0.35);color:var(--text-muted);">"It was there before the Church named anything. That is the only thing every account agrees on. After that, nothing." — Unattributed. Source document: destroyed.</div>
          </div>
        </div>

        <div class="ms-warning" style="border-color:rgba(140,100,180,0.25);background:rgba(30,15,50,0.3);">
          <div class="ms-warning-icon">???</div>
          <div class="ms-warning-content">
            <div class="ms-warning-title">Mind State Effect</div>
            <div class="ms-warning-text">Unverified. Accounts that reference travelers approaching the region describe behavioral changes that were not attributed to the region at the time of writing. In retrospect, the pattern is suggestive. No controlled data exists.</div>
          </div>
        </div>
      </div>


    </section>

(function() {
  const c = document.getElementById('stars-container');
  if (!c) return;
  for (let i = 0; i < 70; i++) {
    const s = document.createElement('div');
    s.className = 'star';
    s.style.cssText = `left:${Math.random()*100}%;top:${Math.random()*100}%;--d:${3+Math.random()*5}s;--o:${0.2+Math.random()*0.5};animation-delay:${Math.random()*6}s;width:${Math.random()<0.15?2:1}px;height:${Math.random()<0.15?2:1}px;`;
    c.appendChild(s);
  }
})();

const inp = document.getElementById('search-input');
const ovr = document.getElementById('search-results-overlay');

function doSearch() {
  const q = inp.value.trim().toLowerCase();
  ovr.innerHTML = '';
  if (!q || q.length < 2) { ovr.classList.remove('visible'); return; }
  const results = searchData.filter(i =>
    i.title.toLowerCase().includes(q) ||
    i.excerpt.toLowerCase().includes(q) ||
    i.section.toLowerCase().includes(q)
  ).slice(0, 10);
  if (!results.length) {
    ovr.innerHTML = '<div class="search-no-result">No results found.</div>';
  } else {
    results.forEach(item => {
      const d = document.createElement('div');
      d.className = 'search-result-item';
      d.innerHTML = `<div class="sr-title">${hl(item.title, q)} <span style="font-size:9px;color:var(--text-dim);margin-left:6px;">${item.section}</span></div><div class="sr-excerpt">${hl(item.excerpt.slice(0,80)+'…', q)}</div>`;
      d.onclick = () => {
        const target = document.querySelector(item.anchor);
        if (target) { target.scrollIntoView({ behavior: 'smooth', block: 'start' }); }
        ovr.classList.remove('visible');
      };
      ovr.appendChild(d);
    });
  }
  ovr.classList.add('visible');
}

function hl(text, q) {
  const i = text.toLowerCase().indexOf(q);
  if (i < 0) return esc(text);
  return esc(text.slice(0,i)) + '<mark class="search-highlight">' + esc(text.slice(i,i+q.length)) + '</mark>' + esc(text.slice(i+q.length));
}
function esc(s) { return s.replace(/&/g,'&amp;').replace(/</g,'&lt;').replace(/>/g,'&gt;').replace(/"/g,'&quot;'); }

inp.addEventListener('input', doSearch);
inp.addEventListener('keydown', e => { if (e.key === 'Escape') { ovr.classList.remove('visible'); inp.blur(); } });
document.addEventListener('click', e => {
  if (!e.target.closest('.search-wrap') && !e.target.closest('#search-results-overlay')) ovr.classList.remove('visible');
});

document.querySelectorAll('a[href^="#"]').forEach(a => {
  a.addEventListener('click', e => {
    const t = document.querySelector(a.getAttribute('href'));
    if (t) { e.preventDefault(); t.scrollIntoView({ behavior: 'smooth', block: 'start' }); }
  });
});

const obs = new IntersectionObserver(entries => {
  entries.forEach(e => {
    if (e.isIntersecting) e.target.classList.add('visible');
  });
}, { threshold: 0.06, rootMargin: '0px 0px -30px 0px' });
document.querySelectorAll('.reveal').forEach(el => obs.observe(el));

document.querySelectorAll('.region-index-card').forEach(card => {
  card.addEventListener('click', () => {
    const href = card.getAttribute('href');
    if (href) {
      const t = document.querySelector(href);
      if (t) t.scrollIntoView({ behavior: 'smooth', block: 'start' });
    }
  });
});
</script>
</body>
</html>

    <div class="sidebar-ms-widget">
      <div class="ms-label">Mind State</div>
